comments.text: Up to lower order errors of $O(k)$, the number to be counted is the number of primes between $k$ and $n$ that divide an element of $(n-k,n]$, thanks to Kummer's formula. The problem is somewhat comparable to a prime number theorem for short intervals $[x,x+x^\varepsilon]$.

A partial observation: combining Younis’ short-interval asymptotic for $\Psi(x+h,y)-\Psi(x,y)$ with the fact that for $k>\sqrt n$, $\omega_{>k}!\binom{n}{k}$ is exactly the number of non-$k$-smooth integers in $(n-k,n]$, seems to give $\omega!\binom{n}{k}=(1+o_\delta(1)),k\sum_{k<p<n}\frac1p$ uniformly for $n^{17/30+\delta}\le k\le n^{1-\delta}$. I have not been able to find this specific subrange written down explicitly in the literature or on this page. So unless I am missing a reference, #685 appears to be settled at least in that upper range by existing smooth-number method.

I think you were trying to write LaTeX using parentheses ( ) rather than \$. I have tried to fix this, but you may want to edit your comment further if this is not what you intended. In future please markup inline LaTeX by \$.

There seem to be some punctuation issues with your comment (and it is worth recalling what $\Psi$, $\omega_{>k}$, and $\omega$ mean here). Perhaps also providing a link to Younis's paper would be helpful.
